showcase.extracted_claim: Creatine supplementation is safe for healthy adults.

verdict.summary: Creatine monohydrate is one of the most studied supplements ever; long- and short-term use is safe in healthy adults at standard doses.
verdict.effect_size: no clinically meaningful adverse effects in healthy users
verdict.why: Multiple position stands and reviews — including the ISSN's 2021 position stand and Kreider et al.'s 2017 review of 70+ years of research — find no evidence of kidney or liver damage, dehydration, cramping, or hair loss in healthy users at standard 3-5 g/day doses. The most common 'side effect' is small weight gain from intracellular water.

evidence.source_title: Antonio et al. — ISSN position stand: creatine safety and efficacy
evidence.relevance_note: Most authoritative safety statement; addresses every common myth.

evidence.source_title: Kreider et al. — Safety and efficacy of creatine supplementation (review)
evidence.relevance_note: Reviews 70+ years of safety data; no kidney/liver damage at standard doses.

evidence.source_title: NIH ODS — Creatine fact sheet
evidence.relevance_note: Government fact sheet, lists side effects honestly; nothing serious for healthy users.

showcase.extracted_claim: Creatine damages the kidneys or liver in healthy adults.

verdict.summary: Creatine does not damage kidneys or liver in healthy adults at standard doses; the myth comes from elevated creatinine readings being mistaken for kidney injury.
verdict.effect_size: no documented kidney or liver damage in healthy users
verdict.why: Creatine supplementation raises serum creatinine (a normal breakdown product) which can look like a kidney issue on a lab panel, but renal function (eGFR, cystatin C) stays normal. Long-term trials up to 5 years at 30 g/day in healthy individuals find no organ damage.

evidence.source_title: Kreider et al. — Safety and efficacy of creatine supplementation (review)
evidence.relevance_note: Explicitly addresses kidney/liver myths; no damage found.

evidence.source_title: Antonio et al. — ISSN position stand: creatine safety and efficacy
evidence.relevance_note: Position stand confirms renal safety at standard doses.

showcase.extracted_claim: Creatine causes subcutaneous bloat and visible water retention.

verdict.summary: Creatine increases intracellular water (inside muscle cells), not subcutaneous bloat. The early weight gain is muscle hydration, not 'puffiness'.
verdict.effect_size: +1-2 kg intracellular water in first weeks
verdict.dose: 3-5 g/day; loading 20 g/day for 5-7 days accelerates
verdict.why: The water creatine pulls in is intramuscular — under the skin, inside the cell. Subcutaneous water retention (the 'puffy' look) is not a documented creatine effect at standard doses. After the first 2-4 weeks the visible weight gain stabilizes.

evidence.source_title: Antonio et al. — ISSN position stand: creatine safety and efficacy
evidence.relevance_note: Documents intracellular vs subcutaneous water distinction.

evidence.source_title: Kreider et al. — Safety and efficacy of creatine supplementation (review)
evidence.relevance_note: Quantifies typical 1-2 kg early intracellular water gain.

showcase.extracted_claim: Creatine improves cognitive performance, especially when sleep-deprived or stressed.

verdict.summary: Creatine produces small but real cognitive benefits, with the largest effects in sleep-deprived, stressed, or vegetarian populations.
verdict.effect_size: small effects on working memory and reasoning; larger under sleep deprivation
verdict.dose: 5 g/day; some cognitive studies use 10-20 g/day acutely
verdict.population: adults, with bigger effects in vegetarians and sleep-deprived
verdict.why: Meta-analyses on creatine and cognition find small but consistent benefits, most pronounced in vegetarians (lower baseline brain creatine) and in stress states like sleep deprivation. Effects in well-fed, well-rested omnivores are real but modest.

evidence.source_title: Avgerinos et al. — Creatine and cognitive function (meta-analysis)
evidence.relevance_note: Small but significant pooled cognitive benefit.

evidence.source_title: Antonio et al. — ISSN position stand: creatine safety and efficacy
evidence.relevance_note: Notes emerging cognitive applications.

evidence.source_title: Gordji-Nejad et al. — Single dose creatine improves cognition under sleep deprivation
evidence.relevance_note: Demonstrated acute cognitive rescue from a single dose.

showcase.extracted_claim: Creatine supplementation causes hair loss in healthy adult men.

verdict.summary: Evidence that creatine causes hair loss is weak and based largely on a single small DHT study that did not measure hair loss.
verdict.effect_size: no measurable effect on hair documented
verdict.population: studied in young men; no hair-loss outcomes
verdict.why: The 'creatine causes hair loss' claim traces back to one 2009 RCT in rugby players that found a rise in DHT, but the study did not measure hair loss itself. Subsequent reviews and meta-analyses have not replicated the DHT finding, and no trial has shown increased shedding or AGA progression.

evidence.source_title: van der Merwe et al. — Creatine and DHT in rugby players
evidence.relevance_note: Origin of the DHT claim; did not measure hair loss.

evidence.source_title: Antonio et al. — ISSN position stand: creatine safety and efficacy
evidence.relevance_note: Concludes creatine has no documented adverse effect on hair.

evidence.source_title: NIH ODS — Dietary Supplements for Exercise and Athletic Performance: Creatine
evidence.relevance_note: Government fact sheet listing established side effects; hair loss not among them.

showcase.extracted_claim: Ashwagandha increases testosterone by 40% in healthy adult men.

verdict.summary: Ashwagandha shows small testosterone increases mainly in stressed or infertile men; a 40% boost in healthy lifters is not supported.
verdict.effect_size: ~10-15% in stressed/infertile men; minimal in healthy
verdict.population: studied in stressed or infertile adult men
verdict.why: Multiple small RCTs show modest testosterone increases in stressed or sub-fertile men taking standardized ashwagandha for 8+ weeks. Effects in healthy, well-trained men are smaller and inconsistent, and no trial supports a 40% increase. The claim overstates a real but limited effect.

evidence.source_title: Lopresti et al. — Ashwagandha and hormonal balance in overweight men
evidence.relevance_note: Found ~14% testosterone increase vs placebo.

evidence.source_title: Ambiye et al. — Ashwagandha in oligospermic men
evidence.relevance_note: Significant testosterone increase but in non-healthy population.

evidence.source_title: Wankhede et al. — Ashwagandha and resistance training
evidence.relevance_note: Small testosterone effect (~15%) alongside training; not 40%.

showcase.extracted_claim: BCAAs build muscle in adults eating adequate protein.

verdict.summary: BCAAs add little or no muscle growth when total daily protein intake is already adequate.
verdict.why: Muscle protein synthesis requires all essential amino acids. Studies isolating BCAAs show transient signaling effects but no advantage over whole protein. When daily protein meets ~1.6 g/kg, BCAAs add nothing measurable.

evidence.source_title: Wolfe — BCAAs do not stimulate muscle protein synthesis alone
evidence.relevance_note: Mechanistic review concluding BCAAs alone are insufficient.

evidence.source_title: Jackman et al. — BCAA ingestion and post-exercise MPS
evidence.relevance_note: BCAAs raise MPS less than whole protein.

evidence.source_title: Morton et al. — Protein intake and resistance training meta-analysis
evidence.relevance_note: Total protein, not BCAAs, drives hypertrophy.

verdict.summary: Beta-alanine moderately improves performance in efforts lasting 1-4 minutes by buffering muscle acidosis.
verdict.why: Multiple RCTs and an ISSN position stand support beta-alanine for tasks limited by muscle acidosis. Benefit is largest in the 60-240s window; effects on max strength or short sprints are smaller.

evidence.source_title: Trexler et al. — ISSN position stand on beta-alanine
evidence.relevance_note: Strong evidence for 1-4 min exercise tasks.

evidence.source_title: Hobson et al. — Beta-alanine meta-analysis
evidence.relevance_note: Pooled ~2.85% improvement in target window.

evidence.source_title: Saunders et al. — Beta-alanine effects updated meta
evidence.relevance_note: Confirms moderate effect size.

verdict.summary: Caffeine reliably improves strength and power output across many populations and dosing protocols.
verdict.why: An ISSN position stand and several meta-analyses converge on a clear, reproducible ergogenic effect of caffeine for strength, power, and endurance. Effects are robust across genotypes with modest individual variation.

evidence.source_title: Guest et al. — ISSN position stand: caffeine and exercise performance
evidence.relevance_note: Strong recommendation for strength/power.

evidence.source_title: Grgic et al. — Caffeine and muscle strength meta-analysis
evidence.relevance_note: Significant pooled improvement in 1RM.

evidence.source_title: Grgic — Caffeine and Wingate test meta-analysis
evidence.relevance_note: Confirms power benefit.

showcase.extracted_claim: Tongkat ali boosts testosterone 200% in healthy men.

verdict.summary: Tongkat ali shows modest testosterone restoration in hypogonadal or stressed men; a 200% increase in healthy men is not supported.
verdict.effect_size: small effect, mostly in low-T or stressed populations
verdict.population: studied mainly in older or hypogonadal men
verdict.why: Available RCTs report partial testosterone normalization in men with low baseline levels but no dramatic increases in healthy lifters. Studies are small and often industry-funded.

evidence.source_title: Tambi et al. — Tongkat ali in late-onset hypogonadism
evidence.relevance_note: Restoration toward normal range, not a 200% boost.

evidence.source_title: Talbott et al. — Tongkat ali and stress hormones
evidence.relevance_note: Modest hormonal shifts in stressed cohort.

evidence.source_title: Examine-style review of tongkat ali (NIH ODS adjacent)
evidence.relevance_note: Notes lack of large trials in healthy men.

showcase.extracted_claim: You need 1 gram of protein per pound of bodyweight to build muscle.

verdict.summary: Above roughly 1.6 g/kg/day (~0.7 g/lb), extra protein produces no additional muscle gain. The popular '1 g per pound' rule is conservative but not necessary.
verdict.dose: 1.4-2.0 g/kg/day is sufficient for most athletes
verdict.why: A 2018 meta-analysis pooling 49 RCTs (n=1,863) identified a plateau in fat-free mass gains around 1.6 g/kg/day. The ISSN position stand recommends 1.4-2.0 g/kg/day, well below the '1 g per pound' (2.2 g/kg) target. Eating more isn't harmful but isn't required for hypertrophy.

evidence.source_title: Morton et al. — Protein intake and resistance training meta-analysis
evidence.relevance_note: Identified ~1.6 g/kg/day plateau.

evidence.source_title: Jäger et al. — ISSN position stand: protein and exercise
evidence.relevance_note: Recommends 1.4-2.0 g/kg/day for most athletes.

evidence.source_title: Schoenfeld et al. — Nutrient timing and the anabolic window
evidence.relevance_note: Total daily intake matters more than timing or extreme totals.

showcase.extracted_claim: Fasted cardio burns more fat than fed cardio.

verdict.summary: Fasted cardio shifts acute fuel use toward fat but does not produce more long-term fat loss when total calories are matched.
verdict.effect_size: no meaningful body composition difference vs fed cardio
verdict.why: Schoenfeld's 4-week RCT in 20 dieting women found identical fat and lean-mass changes between fasted and fed steady-state cardio. The 'fat oxidation' fasted cardio is famous for refers to substrate use during the bout, not 24-hour or weekly fat balance.

evidence.source_title: Schoenfeld et al. — Body composition with fasted vs fed aerobic exercise
evidence.relevance_note: No body composition advantage to fasted cardio when calories matched.

showcase.extracted_claim: Intermittent fasting produces more fat loss than regular calorie restriction.

verdict.summary: Intermittent fasting is not metabolically superior to standard calorie restriction; effects are largely explained by reduced total intake.
verdict.effect_size: no metabolic advantage when calories are matched
verdict.why: Reviews and trials such as the Lowe et al. 2020 TREAT study show only small differences vs continuous restriction. Lowe found ~1 kg additional loss in the 16:8 group, with about 65% coming from lean mass. IF may help adherence for some but isn't a unique fat-loss mechanism.

evidence.source_title: Tinsley & Bounty — Intermittent fasting and body composition review
evidence.relevance_note: Effects explained by total calorie intake.

evidence.source_title: Lowe et al. — TREAT randomized clinical trial: 16:8 TRE
evidence.relevance_note: Only modest loss; ~65% from lean mass.

showcase.extracted_claim: You must eat protein within 30 minutes of training to build muscle.

verdict.summary: The rigid 30-minute post-workout 'anabolic window' is overstated. Total daily protein and calories dominate long-term hypertrophy.
verdict.effect_size: no advantage to extreme post-workout timing in trained adults
verdict.why: Schoenfeld's narrative review on nutrient timing concludes that as long as protein is consumed within a few hours surrounding training (pre or post), exact timing has little measurable effect on hypertrophy in trained individuals.

evidence.source_title: Schoenfeld et al. — Nutrient timing and the anabolic window
evidence.relevance_note: Window is wider than 30-60 minutes.

evidence.source_title: Jäger et al. — ISSN position stand: protein and exercise
evidence.relevance_note: Recommends distributing protein across meals, not racing the clock.

showcase.extracted_claim: You can spot-reduce fat from a specific body area with targeted exercise.

verdict.summary: Targeted exercise does not preferentially remove subcutaneous fat from the trained area.
verdict.why: Kostek et al.'s 12-week unilateral arm training study used DEXA imaging and found similar fat reductions in trained and untrained limbs. Fat-loss patterns are determined primarily by overall energy deficit and individual physiology, not by which muscle worked.

evidence.source_title: Kostek et al. — Subcutaneous fat alterations with unilateral resistance training
evidence.relevance_note: Trained arm did not lose more fat than untrained arm.

showcase.extracted_claim: Training each muscle twice a week is better than once a week for hypertrophy.

verdict.summary: Training each muscle group twice per week produces greater hypertrophy than once per week when weekly volume is equated.
verdict.dose: equal weekly hard-set count, distributed across 2+ sessions
verdict.why: Schoenfeld's 2016 meta-analysis on training frequency found that, with weekly sets matched, 2x/week split outperformed 1x/week. Going from 2x to 3x produced little additional benefit. Volume matters more than frequency per se.

evidence.source_title: Schoenfeld et al. — Resistance training frequency meta-analysis
evidence.relevance_note: Twice/week beats once/week with matched volume.

showcase.extracted_claim: Lactic acid causes muscle soreness the day after training.

verdict.summary: Lactic acid does not cause delayed-onset muscle soreness. Lactate is cleared within an hour.
verdict.why: Cheung et al. and Cairns review the mechanisms: DOMS arises from mechanical micro-trauma during unaccustomed eccentric loading, not metabolic acidosis. By the time soreness peaks 24-72h later, lactate is long gone.

evidence.source_title: Cheung et al. — Delayed onset muscle soreness review
evidence.relevance_note: Mechanical micro-trauma, not lactic acid, causes DOMS.

evidence.source_title: Cairns — Lactic acid and exercise performance review
evidence.relevance_note: Reframes lactate as fuel, not fatigue toxin.
